narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 48% chance that you would fail it—high enough to trigger rejection as we prioritize admitting students with stronger projected outcomes. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, particularly your position in Year 3 where you ranked in the second decile among your peers, far below the typical fifth or sixth decile spot of students who go on to pass the bar. That lower Year 3 standing was the single biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as it signals potential challenges in sustaining strong performance through law school and beyond. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 played a counterbalancing role, nearly matching the 3.24 average among bar passers and helping pull the prediction toward success. Similarly, your LSAT score of 37 provided solid support in the model's eyes, aligning closely with the 37.27 average for those who pass, which tempered the overall risk assessment. While your high family income and full-time study plans are favorable—mirroring patterns among successful students like the 93% who study full-time and the upper-income brackets that perform well—they weren't the dominant drivers here. Compared to admitted applicants, your profile stands out for that Year 3 decile dip, which outweighed your otherwise competitive test scores and background. The model doesn't consider gender or race in its decisions, focusing purely on bar passage predictors like these. This close 48% call means small improvements could shift future outcomes dramatically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted prep to boost your class ranking, perhaps seeking tutoring or academic advising to address any Year 3 hurdles. Reapplying after strengthening that area, maybe with updated transcripts, could present a more compelling case. You're clearly capable, as shown by your LSAT and GPA—building on those strengths will make a real difference next time.
